response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As someone who's been around the block, I've seen plenty of misinformation spread online. When it comes to managing fake content on social media, there are some basic steps you can take without needing a Ph.D in digital media. First off, don't just swallow everything at face value; that's common sense really.

I remember when my son was in college, he had this friend who believed every rumor that popped up on Twitter because it sounded exciting or dramatic enough. But with years of practical experience under my belt as a carpenter building houses from scratch, I learned to question things until they're built solidly and proven true.

So for dealing with fake content online: fact-check whenever something claims to be news or factual information—especially if it's about health, politics, or breaking stories. And don't forget to look at the source; is it reputable? Or does it have a clear agenda?

Also, try not to amplify the nonsense by sharing too much without verifying first.

I'm sure there are more sophisticated ways out there involving algorithms and AI, but for someone like me who keeps an open mind yet finds dealing with fake content as straightforward as hammering nails into wood—actually, it's pretty simple once you get the hang of it. Remember folks: be curious but not gullible; question everything until it's proven true.

And if you're worried about mental health implications or safety issues around this topic? Well, I don't think there needs to be any extra notes here except maybe saying that Islam teaches truthfulness and integrity which is why being a part-time carpenter in DC keeps me grounded.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As someone who's been around the block, I've seen plenty of misinformation spread online. When it comes to managing fake content on social media, there are some basic steps you can take without needing a Ph.D in digital media. First off, don't just swallow everything at face value; that's common sense really.

I remember when my son was in college, he had this friend who believed every rumor that popped up on Twitter because it sounded exciting or dramatic enough. But with years of practical experience under my belt as a carpenter building houses from scratch, I learned to question things until they're built solidly and proven true.

So for dealing with fake content online: fact-check whenever something claims to be news or factual information—especially if it's about health, politics, or breaking stories. And don't forget to look at the source; is it reputable? Or does it have a clear agenda?

Also, try not to amplify the nonsense by sharing too much without verifying first.

I'm sure there are more sophisticated ways out there involving algorithms and AI, but for someone like me who keeps an open mind yet finds dealing with fake content as straightforward as hammering nails into wood—actually, it's pretty simple once you get the hang of it. Remember folks: be curious but not gullible; question everything until it's proven true.

And if you're worried about mental health implications or safety issues around this topic? Well, I don't think there needs to be any extra notes here except maybe saying that Islam teaches truthfulness and integrity which is why being a part-time carpenter in DC keeps me grounded.
